content_html:
INF10025 Data Management and AnalyticsLecture 7 – Part 1
Composite Keys
Weak Entities

Natural Keys recap
We have discussed the notion of Natural Keys
If an organisation does not have an Employee No associated with each employee, then we could use employee name as a PK
EMPLOYEE(EmpName, PhoneNo, PayRate)
TIMELOG(WeekNo, HoursWorked, EmpName)
FK (EmpName) references EMPLOYEE

Composite Keys
We have also discussed the notion of Composite Keys.
This is where the Primary Key is made up of values from multiple columns
EMPLOYEE(GivenName, Surname, PhoneNo, PayRate)

Composite Keys
When the Primary Key is made up of values from multiple columns, then any Foreign Key must also be an identical match for the Primary Key
EMPLOYEE(GivenName, Surname, PhoneNo, PayRate)
TIMELOG(WeekNo, HoursWorked, GivenName, Surname)
FK (GivenName, Surname) references EMPLOYEE

Strong and Weak entities
An entity is a person, place, object, event or concept.
Entities can be classified into two groups
Strong Entities
Weak Entities
So far we have only focused on Strong Entities
A strong entity is :
"An entity type that exists independently on other entity types"
– Hoffer Prescott McFadden
"An entity type that is not existent-dependent on some other entity type"     	– Connolly & Begg
Therefore a strong entity can be identified by its own attributes. That is a identifier can be chosen from its own attributes
For example, BUILDING is a strong entity

Strong and Weak entities
Consider the buildings at Swinburne University. E.g. The BA Building
It has a building code: BA
It has a description: Business Arts
The Building entity is a strong entity

Weak Entities
We also know the following information about a room. e.g.:
RoomNo	403
RoomDesc	Lecture Theatre
Capacity	85
Building	BA
Our two Entities might look like this:
Rule of ERDs:
An attribute must only appear once in an ERD

Borrowed Identifiers
There is a relationship between Room and Building
The diagram shows that a Room must be contained in one building.
"One Room Must be Contained in One Building"
"One Building May Contain Many Rooms"
We can easily 'navigate' the relationship to the building code.

Borrowed Identifiers
Is the attribute RoomNo enough to identify a room?    NO!
Swinburne has many rooms with RoomNo 403.(403 in the EN bldg, and 403 in the ATC bldg…)
What then, should the identifier be?
A composite identifier
The Identifier for Room must be BuildingCode + RoomNo   (e.g.  BA 403)
Problem:
BuildingCode is not an attribute of ROOM.

Borrowed Identifiers
How do we indicate that the identifier is borrowed?
The relationship is part of the identifier,so we must underline the relationship name
The entity at the MANY end of the relationship, uses the identifier at the ONE end of the relationship as part of its identifier.
The underlined relationship name means:
The identifier of ROOM is: BuildingCode + RoomNo

Underlined relationship name
The underline is important

Weak Entities
A weak entity is
" …an entity type that borrows part of, or all of its … identifier…" - Mannino
Thus, ROOM is a WEAK entity.
The identifier of Room is partially or fully defined by identifier from other related entities.
WEAK entities are drawn with a double outline

Weak Entities
What does the ERD below tell us?:
RoomNo is underlined. It must be part of the identifier of ROOM
The double outline indicates that the ROOM is a weak entity
So part of the ROOM's identifier must be 'borrowed'.
The relationship named 'contains' is underlined
So the identifier at the many endborrows the identifier from the one end of the relationship
The Identifier of Room  is BuildingCode + RoomNo

Revised ERD – RM Conversion
An ERD with a one-to-many relationship can be converted to a Relational Schema using the following steps:
Create a table for each entity	  BUILDING,        ROOM
Make each attribute a column
BUILDING ( BuildingCode, Description )
ROOM (RoomNo, Capacity )
For each strong entity, choose an identifier as a PRIMARY KEY	   BUILDING ( BuildingCode, Description )

Revised ERD – RM Conversion
Take the Primary Key at the one end of the relationship andmake it into a Foreign Key at the many end.         BUILDING ( BuildingCode, Description)        ROOM (RoomNo, BuildingCode, Capacity)
If the entity at the many end uses a borrowed identifer, then make the foreign key part of the PRIMARY KEY.        BUILDING ( BuildingCode, Description)        ROOM (RoomNo, BuildingCode, Capacity)

ERD – RM Conversion
By convention, we tend to list the borrowed attribute first.
ROOM ( BuildingCode, RoomNo, Capacity )
BUILDING ( BuildingCode, Description )
FK

FK key is also composite
If a foreign key references a PK which is a composite key, then the FK must be a composite
COMPUTER (Compid, Description, BuildingCode, RoomNo)
Primary Key (Compid)
Foreign Key (BuildingCode, RoomNo) references ROOM
COMPUTER
CompID
Description
Location for

SQL if composite keys are used
When composite PK and FKs are used, then the ON clause requires all columns of the PK & FK to be specified
SELECT *
FROM ROOM RINNER JOIN COMPUTER C
ON C.BuildingCode = R.BuildingCode AND C.RoomNo = R.RoomNo
